# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each module typically carries **Part A (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks)** and **Part B (Internal Choice: Answer ONE out of TWO × 14 marks = 14 marks)**. Numerical problems in Part B usually carry 7 marks each.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Economic Order Quantity" (EOQ). State any four assumptions of the basic EOQ model.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) is the **fixed order quantity that minimizes the total annual inventory cost** (sum of ordering and holding costs) under conditions of constant demand, fixed lead time, and instantaneous replenishment. It is also known as **Wilson's Lot Size Formula**, given by $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_c}}$.

**Four Assumptions:**
1. Demand rate is constant and known (deterministic).
2. Lead time is constant and known.
3. Order is received in a single delivery (no partial shipments).
4. Ordering cost is fixed per order; holding cost is proportional to average inventory.

*(Any four valid assumptions — full 3 marks)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between P-System and Q-System of inventory control.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Q-System (Continuous Review) | P-System (Periodic Review) |
|---|---|---|
| Review Frequency | Continuous (real-time monitoring) | Periodic (fixed interval $P$) |
| Order Quantity | Fixed ($Q = Q^*$) | Variable ($Q_i = T - I_i$) |
| Trigger | When stock hits ROL | At end of every period $P$ |
| Record Keeping | Detailed, transaction-by-transaction | Simple, periodic updates |
| Best Suited For | High-value 'A' items | Group ordering / multiple items |
| Safety Stock | Lower | Higher (covers $P+L$ variability) |

*(Tabular comparison — 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Choice A) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 - 14 Marks]`

**A manufacturing company uses 18,000 units of a raw material per year. The ordering cost is ₹250 per order and the holding cost is ₹6 per unit per year. The supplier offers the following quantity discount schedule:**

| Order Quantity | Unit Price (₹) |
|---|---|
| 1 – 499 | 20.00 |
| 500 – 999 | 19.50 |
| 1000 and above | 19.00 |

**Calculate the optimal order quantity. Also, determine the number of orders per year and the total annual cost.**

#### Part (a) — Basic EOQ Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate EOQ at the lowest price level (₹19.00).**

$$Q_3 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 18{,}000 \cdot 250}{6}} = \sqrt{\frac{9{,}000{,}000}{6}} = \sqrt{1{,}500{,}000} \approx 1224.7 \text{ units}$$

Since 1224.7 ≥ 1000, this EOQ **is feasible** at the ₹19.00 price break. We still check higher breaks to be safe.

**Step 2: Calculate EOQ at the middle price level (₹19.50).**

$$Q_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 18{,}000 \cdot 250}{6}} = \sqrt{1{,}500{,}000} \approx 1224.7 \text{ units}$$

Since 1224.7 is **NOT in the 500–999 range**, this is **infeasible**. Adjust to $Q = 1000$ (next breakpoint).

**Step 3: Calculate EOQ at the highest price level (₹20.00).**

$$Q_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 18{,}000 \cdot 250}{6}} \approx 1224.7 \text{ units}$$

Since 1224.7 > 499, **infeasible**. Adjust to $Q = 500$ (next breakpoint).

#### Part (b) — Total Cost Comparison (7 Marks)

**Total Cost Formula:** $TC = \frac{D}{Q} C_o + \frac{Q}{2} C_c + D \cdot V$

**Option 1: Q = 1225 units @ ₹19.00**

$$TC_1 = \frac{18{,}000}{1225} \cdot 250 + \frac{1225}{2} \cdot 6 + 18{,}000 \cdot 19$$

$$TC_1 = 3673.47 + 3675 + 3{,}42{,}000 = ₹3{,}49{,}348.47$$

**Option 2: Q = 1000 units @ ₹19.00**

$$TC_2 = \frac{18{,}000}{1000} \cdot 250 + \frac{1000}{2} \cdot 6 + 18{,}000 \cdot 19$$

$$TC_2 = 4500 + 3000 + 3{,}42{,}000 = ₹3{,}49{,}500$$

**Option 3: Q = 500 units @ ₹19.50**

$$TC_3 = \frac{18{,}000}{500} \cdot 250 + \frac{500}{2} \cdot 6 + 18{,}000 \cdot 19.50$$

$$TC_3 = 9000 + 1500 + 3{,}51{,}000 = ₹3{,}61{,}500$$

**Decision:** $TC_1 = ₹3{,}49{,}348.47$ is minimum.

$$\boxed{Q^* = 1225 \text{ units, Number of orders} = \frac{18{,}000}{1225} \approx 15 \text{ orders/year}}$$

---

### Question B2 (Choice B - Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 - 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Define Materials Requirement Planning (MRP). List its three primary inputs and explain each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A component has annual demand of 24,000 units, ordering cost of ₹300 per order, and holding cost of ₹4 per unit per year. Lead time is 8 days, and standard deviation of daily demand is 6 units. The firm desires a 95% service level (Z = 1.65). Calculate: **(i)** EOQ, **(ii)** Safety Stock, **(iii)** Reorder Level, **(iv)** Total Annual Inventory Cost (excluding purchase), and **(v)** Time Between Orders. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — MRP Definition and Inputs (7 Marks)

**Definition (3 marks):**
**Materials Requirement Planning (MRP)** is a computer-based production planning and inventory control system designed to manage **dependent demand items**. It translates the Master Production Schedule (MPS) into a time-phased schedule specifying when and how many components and raw materials should be ordered to meet production requirements while minimizing inventory.

**Three Primary Inputs (4 marks):**

1. **Master Production Schedule (MPS):**
   A detailed plan stating **what finished products are to be produced, in what quantities, and by when**. It drives all subsequent material planning. Example: "Produce 500 units of Product X in Week 4."

2. **Bill of Materials (BOM):**
   A structured list of all raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, and their quantities required to manufacture one unit of the finished product. Often visualized as a **product structure tree**, with the finished good at Level 0 and raw materials at the lowest levels.

3. **Inventory Status File:**
   A real-time database recording the **current on-hand quantities, quantities already on order (scheduled receipts), quantities allocated to existing orders, lead times for each item, and safety stock levels** for every component. This file is updated after every transaction.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 24{,}000$ units/year
- $C_o = ₹300$/order
- $C_c = ₹4$/unit/year
- $L = 8$ days
- $\sigma_d = 6$ units/day
- $Z = 1.65$ (95% service)
- Working days = 300/year → $d = 24{,}000/300 = 80$ units/day

**(i) EOQ:**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 24{,}000 \cdot 300}{4}} = \sqrt{3{,}600{,}000} = 1897.37 \approx 1897 \text{ units}$$

**(ii) Safety Stock:**

$$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L} = 1.65 \cdot 6 \cdot \sqrt{8} = 1.65 \cdot 6 \cdot 2.828 = 28.0 \text{ units}$$

**(iii) Reorder Level:**

$$ROL = d \cdot L + SS = 80 \cdot 8 + 28 = 640 + 28 = 668 \text{ units}$$

**(iv) Total Annual Inventory Cost (excluding purchase):**

$$TC = \frac{D}{Q^*} \cdot C_o + \frac{Q^*}{2} \cdot C_c$$

$$TC = \frac{24{,}000}{1897.37} \cdot 300 + \frac{1897.37}{2} \cdot 4$$

$$TC = 12{,}649.11 + 3794.74 = ₹16{,}443.85/\text{year}$$

**(v) Time Between Orders:**

$$\text{Number of orders} = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{24{,}000}{1897.37} = 12.65$$

$$TBO = \frac{300 \text{ working days}}{12.65} = 23.72 \text{ days/order}$$

$$\boxed{Q^* = 1897 \text{ units, } SS = 28 \text{ units, } ROL = 668 \text{ units, } TC = ₹16{,}444/\text{year, } TBO = 23.72 \text{ days}}$$

---

### Question B3 (Practice Bank - 14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the Just-In-Time (JIT) system in detail. Discuss its key elements and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **ABC, VED, FSN, SDE, and HML** techniques of selective inventory control. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — JIT Detailed (7 Marks)

**Definition:** Just-In-Time (JIT) is a Japanese-origin (Toyota Production System) **pull-based** manufacturing philosophy that produces **only what is needed, only when it is needed, and only in the quantity needed**, thereby minimizing inventory levels and associated costs.

**Key Elements (5 marks):**
1. **Pull System (Kanban):** Production driven by actual downstream demand, using visual cards (kanbans) as signals. No production without a kanban.
2. **Small Lot Sizes:** Reduces WIP and inventory carrying costs.
3. **Short Setup Times (SMED):** Single-Minute Exchange of Dies enables economic small batches.
4. **Total Quality Management (TQM) / Jidoka:** Zero defects philosophy — stop production immediately when defects are detected.
5. **Supplier Partnerships:** Few, reliable, geographically close suppliers.
6. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Ongoing, incremental improvements by all employees.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Vulnerable to disruptions** (e.g., natural disasters, pandemics, geopolitical issues).
2. Requires **highly reliable transportation** and **zero-defect discipline**.
3. **High employee stress** due to intense pace and minimal buffers.
4. **Inflexible** to sudden demand surges.

#### Part (b) — Selective Inventory Control Techniques (7 Marks)

| Technique | Classification Criterion | Classes & Use |
|---|---|---|
| **ABC Analysis** | Annual Consumption Value | A (10-20% items, 70-80% value) — tight control; B (20-30%, 15-25%) — moderate; C (50-70%, 5-10%) — loose |
| **VED Analysis** | Criticality of Spare Parts | V (Vital — production halts), E (Essential — slows), D (Desirable — no impact) |
| **FSN Analysis** | Frequency of Issue | F (Fast-moving), S (Slow-moving), N (Non-moving) |
| **SDE Analysis** | Procurement Difficulty | S (Scarce), D (Difficult), E (Easy) |
| **HML Analysis** | Unit Price | H (High — pilferage risk), M (Medium), L (Low) |

*(Tabular coverage of all five — 7 marks)*

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Traps (Based on KTU Board Valuation Patterns):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** A numerical answer like *"EOQ = 1225"* without **"units"** loses 0.5–1 mark. Always write **"EOQ = 1225 units."**
> 
> 2. **Discount Model Mistake:** Using $C_c$ (flat) instead of $C_c \cdot V_j$ (price-dependent) in holding cost term. **The holding cost is a PERCENTAGE of item value, so it changes with each price break.**
> 
> 3. **ROL Omitting Safety Stock:** Writing $ROL = d \cdot L$ only (forgetting SS) loses 2 marks in 7-mark problems. **SS is mandatory unless explicitly stated as zero.**
> 
> 4. **MRP Time-Phasing Errors:** Forgetting to **offset** (subtract lead time) when stating Planned Order Release dates. Always show both **Planned Order Receipt** and **Planned Order Release** rows.
> 
> 5. **Q-System vs. P-System Confusion:** Writing $Q$ (fixed) for P-System or $P$ (fixed) for Q-System. **Verify your variables match the system definition.**
> 
> 6. **JIT Mistakes:** Calling JIT a "push" system (it's **pull**), or stating JIT requires "many suppliers" (it's **few partnerships**).
> 
> 7. **ABC Calculation Errors:** Sorting by **usage only** instead of **usage × unit cost (consumption value)**. ABC is always based on **monetary value**, not quantity.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER 📝

### Quick Revision Checklist

#### MRP
- Three inputs: **MPS, BOM, Inventory Status File**.
- Three steps: **Netting, Lotting, Offsetting**.
- Manages **dependent demand** items.

#### EOQ
- Formula: $Q^* = \sqrt{2DC_o / C_c}$
- **At optimal Q, Ordering Cost = Holding Cost.**
- Basic model assumes **constant demand, no shortages, instantaneous replenishment**.

#### Discount Models
- Use **lowest price** EOQ first; check feasibility.
- If infeasible, **adjust upward** to breakpoint.
- **Compare TC at each feasible Q** — minimum wins.

#### Reorder Level
- $ROL = d \cdot L + SS$
- **Always include Safety Stock** unless told otherwise.
- $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ (basic formula).

#### Q-System vs P-System
- **Q = Quantity fixed, Timing variable** (Continuous review).
- **P = Period fixed, Quantity variable** (Periodic review).

#### Selective Inventory Control
- **ABC** = Consumption Value (₹).
- **VED** = Vitality of Spare Parts.
- **FSN** = Frequency of Movement.
- **SDE** = Procurement Difficulty.
- **HML** = Unit Price.

#### JIT
- **Pull system**, originated in **Toyota (Japan)**.
- Aims for **zero inventory, zero defects, zero waste**.
- Key tools: **Kanban, Kaizen, SMED, Jidoka**.
- Limitations: vulnerable to **supply chain disruptions**.

#### Supply Chain Management
- Five stages: **Supplier → Manufacturer → Warehouse → Retailer → Customer**.
- Two flows: **Forward (materials) + Reverse (returns)**.
- Includes **logistics + information + finance** flows.
- Key concept: **Bullwhip Effect** — demand variability amplifies upstream.

---

### Final Board Exam Tips 🎯

> [!TIP]
> 1. **Always draw diagrams** for MRP tables, ABC curves, and supply chain flows — KTU awards marks for visual clarity.
> 2. **Verify your EOQ answer** by computing AOC = AHC at $Q^*$. If they match, your answer is **correct**.
> 3. **Use 360 or 365 days** consistently when converting annual demand to daily demand — state your assumption.
> 4. **Mention units** in every numerical answer — this is the easiest mark to earn.
> 5. **For ABC classification**, use the **70/20/5** rule unless the question specifies different thresholds.
> 6. **For JIT questions**, mention **Toyota Production System** and **Taiichi Ohno** for full credit.
> 7. **For SCM**, contrast with traditional **logistics** to demonstrate conceptual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Breakdown, Preventive and Predictive Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT614 - PRINCIPLES OF INDUSTRIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Breakdown, Preventive and Predictive Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Maintenance** is the combination of all technical, administrative, and managerial actions during the life cycle of an item intended to retain it in, or restore it to, a state in which it can perform the required function. In industrial management, maintenance is classified into three foundational philosophies: **Breakdown Maintenance (BM)**, **Preventive Maintenance (PM)**, and **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Breakdown Maintenance (BM):** A corrective maintenance strategy performed *after* a machine, equipment, or system has failed or broken down. Also called **"Run-to-Failure"** or **Reactive Maintenance**. The primary objective is to restore the equipment to its operational state as quickly as possible to minimize downtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preventive Maintenance (PM):** A scheduled, time-based maintenance strategy performed *before* a failure occurs. It is based on predetermined intervals (calendar time, operating hours, or cycle counts) regardless of the actual condition of the equipment. Objective: reduce the probability of failure or degradation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Predictive Maintenance (PdM):** A condition-based maintenance strategy that uses real-time monitoring, data acquisition, and diagnostic tools to track equipment health. Maintenance is performed *only when* indicators show that failure is imminent. Objective: perform maintenance exactly when needed, eliminating unnecessary interventions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you own a **car**:

- **Breakdown Maintenance** = You drive until the engine seizes on the highway, then call a tow truck. *(No planning, maximum disruption.)*
- **Preventive Maintenance** = You change your engine oil every 5,000 km whether the car needs it or not, and replace brake pads every 20,000 km as per the service manual. *(Disciplined, scheduled, but sometimes wasteful.)*
- **Predictive Maintenance** = You install sensors that monitor oil viscosity, brake pad thickness, and engine temperature. You service the car only when the sensor data tells you a part is degrading. *(Smart, data-driven, cost-optimal.)*

### 1.3 Standard Maintenance Performance Metrics

The following metrics are universally used in industrial engineering for evaluating maintenance effectiveness:

> [!NOTE]
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average time a system operates between consecutive failures. Expressed in hours.
> - **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Average time required to repair a failed component and restore it to operation. Expressed in hours.
> - **Availability (A):** The fraction of time that a system is in a functioning condition. Expressed as a percentage.
> - **Reliability (R(t)):** The probability that a system will perform its intended function under designated conditions for a specified period of time.

These four metrics form the **golden quartet** of maintenance analytics, and any KTU question on this topic is highly likely to involve one or more of them.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Breakdown Maintenance — Detailed Operational Logic

- **Philosophy:** "If it ain't broke, don't fix it."
- **Trigger Event:** Unplanned equipment failure or performance drop below acceptable threshold.
- **Cost Profile:** Highest in the long run due to:
  - Unplanned production stoppages
  - Secondary damage to adjacent components
  - High overtime labor costs during emergencies
  - Loss of customer goodwill due to delayed deliveries
- **Applicability:** Used for **non-critical** assets where:
  - The cost of preventive inspection exceeds repair cost
  - Redundant systems exist (standby equipment)
  - Failure consequences are negligible (e.g., office printers, low-priority tools)

### 2.2 Preventive Maintenance — Detailed Operational Logic

- **Philosophy:** "An ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure."
- **Trigger Event:** Scheduled calendar (weekly/monthly) or usage interval (every 500 hours of operation).
- **Two Sub-Types:**
  - **Scheduled PM:** Hard-time replacements at fixed intervals regardless of condition.
  - **Opportunistic PM:** Maintenance performed when equipment is already down for other reasons.
- **Cost Profile:** Moderate; trades reduced breakdown costs against scheduled downtime and over-maintenance.
- **Applicability:** Used for **critical** assets where failure consequences are severe but the equipment has well-known degradation patterns.

### 2.3 Predictive Maintenance — Detailed Operational Logic

- **Philosophy:** "Listen to what the equipment is telling you."
- **Trigger Event:** Measured degradation indicators exceeding pre-set thresholds.
- **Core Techniques:**
  - **Vibration Analysis** (rotating machinery, bearings)
  - **Thermography / Infrared Imaging** (electrical panels, motors)
  - **Oil Analysis** (lubricant condition, wear particles)
  - **Ultrasonic Testing** (leaks, electrical discharge)
  - **Acoustic Emission Monitoring** (crack propagation)
- **Cost Profile:** Lowest long-run cost; requires investment in sensors, training, and software.
- **Applicability:** Used for **high-value, critical** assets in petrochemical, power generation, aerospace, and precision manufacturing.

### 2.4 Comparative Strategic Matrix

| Dimension | Breakdown Maintenance | Preventive Maintenance | Predictive Maintenance |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Trigger | Failure occurrence | Time / usage interval | Condition indicator |
| Planning | None | High (calendar) | High (analytics) |
| Downtime | Unplanned, severe | Planned, moderate | Minimal, targeted |
| Initial Cost | Very low | Moderate | High |
| Long-run Cost | Highest | Moderate | Lowest |
| Skill Required | Basic repair | Trained technician | Data analyst + technician |
| Data Dependency | None | Low | Very high |
| Spare Inventory | High (emergency) | Moderate | Low (just-in-time) |
| Suitability | Non-critical assets | General-purpose | Critical assets |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following four formulas are the **most frequently tested equations** in KTU examinations on this topic. Memorize them thoroughly.

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}
$$

$$
\text{Downtime Cost} = \text{MTTR} \times (\text{Lost Production Rate} \times \text{Profit per Unit} + \text{Labor Cost per Hour})
$$

Where the variables have the following meanings:

> - $A$ is the **Availability** of the system (expressed as a percentage from 0 to 100).
> - $\text{MTBF}$ is the **Mean Time Between Failures** in hours.
> - $\text{MTTR}$ is the **Mean Time To Repair** in hours.
> - $R(t)$ is the **Reliability** function — probability of failure-free operation up to time $t$.
> - $\lambda$ is the **Failure Rate** (assumed constant) — failures per hour.
> - $e$ is **Euler's number**, approximately equal to **2.71828**.

### 2.6 Real-World Industrial Utility

These maintenance strategies are foundational in:
- **Manufacturing Plants:** Maximizing Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE).
- **Power Generation:** Ensuring grid reliability in thermal and nuclear plants.
- **Aviation:** Mandatory PdM for jet engines using borescope inspection and oil debris analysis.
- **Oil & Gas:** PdM prevents catastrophic failures in pipelines and offshore platforms.
- **Automotive Industry:** Hybrid PM-PdM models dominate modern assembly lines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Availability Equation

The mathematical foundation of maintenance analysis begins with the steady-state availability equation. We will derive it step by step for full KTU credit.

**Step 1 — Define the concept of steady-state availability.**

In the long run, an equipment alternates between two states: **Operating** and **Down (Under Repair)**. The total cycle time for one operating period plus one repair period equals:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the fraction of time the system is operating.**

Availability is defined as the proportion of total time during which the equipment is in a functional state:

$$
A = \frac{\text{Time Operating}}{\text{Total Time}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage form.**

To express availability as a percentage, multiply by 100:

$$
A\% = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%
$$

This is the standard KTU answer form.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Reliability Function

**Step 1 — Start with the assumption of constant failure rate.**

For many components operating within their useful life (excluding infant mortality and wear-out phases), the failure rate $\lambda$ is approximately constant. This is the **Exponential Distribution** assumption.

**Step 2 — Define the probability of survival over a small interval.**

The probability that a component survives a small time increment $dt$ without failing is:

$$
P(\text{survive in } dt) = 1 - \lambda \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Extend to a finite interval $t$.**

For an interval of length $t$, the survival probability is the product of survival probabilities over $n$ sub-intervals where $n \cdot dt = t$:

$$
R(t) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda t}{n}\right)^n
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the mathematical limit identity.**

Using the standard limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} (1 - x/n)^n = e^{-x}$, we obtain:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 5 — Relate failure rate to MTBF.**

Since $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$, we can rewrite:

$$
R(t) = e^{-t / \text{MTBF}}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

**Problem Statement:** A CNC machine has an MTBF of 800 hours and an MTTR of 8 hours. Compute: (a) the steady-state availability, (b) the reliability over a 100-hour mission, and (c) the expected downtime per year (assuming 24/7 operation, 8760 hours/year).

**Part (a) — Availability Calculation**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{800}{800 + 8} = \frac{800}{808}
$$

$$
A = 0.990099 \approx 99.01\%
$$

**Part (b) — Reliability Calculation**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{800} = 0.00125 \text{ failures/hour}
$$

$$
R(100) = e^{-0.00125 \times 100} = e^{-0.125}
$$

$$
R(100) = 0.8825 \approx 88.25\%
$$

**Part (c) — Annual Downtime Calculation**

$$
\text{Number of Failures per Year} = \frac{8760}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{8760}{800} = 10.95 \text{ failures}
$$

$$
\text{Annual Downtime} = 10.95 \times \text{MTTR} = 10.95 \times 8 = 87.6 \text{ hours/year}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Strategy

Use the following decision logic to select the appropriate maintenance strategy (this is a frequently tested KTU concept):

- **If asset is low-cost AND non-critical AND has no redundancy →** Use **Breakdown Maintenance**.
- **If asset is moderate-cost AND failure has safety/quality implications →** Use **Preventive Maintenance**.
- **If asset is high-cost AND critical AND failure data can be measured →** Use **Predictive Maintenance**.
- **If asset is highly critical AND failure consequences are catastrophic →** Use a **Proactive / Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** hybrid combining all three.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Maintenance Decision System

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the decision logic governing the selection and execution of the three maintenance strategies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Asset Under Management] --> B{Is Asset Critical?}
    B -- No --> C{Is Failure Cost Low?}
    B -- Yes --> D{Can Failure Be Predicted via Sensors?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Apply Breakdown Maintenance]
    C -- No --> F[Apply Preventive Maintenance]
    D -- Yes --> G[Apply Predictive Maintenance]
    D -- No --> H[Apply Preventive Maintenance with Fixed Intervals]
    E --> I[Monitor MTBF and Failure Cost]
    F --> J[Schedule Inspections and Replacements]
    G --> K[Continuous Condition Monitoring Loop]
    H --> J
    I --> L[Review Quarterly for Strategy Upgrade]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{New Criticality Level?}
    M -- Yes --> B
    M -- No --> N[Continue Current Strategy]
```

### 4.2 Comparative Strategy Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StrategyBM["BREAKDOWN MAINTENANCE"]
        bmA[Equipment Operating] --> bmB{Failure Occurs?}
        bmB -- Yes --> bmC[Emergency Repair]
        bmC --> bmD[Restore to Operation]
        bmD --> bmA
        bmB -- No --> bmA
    end
    subgraph StrategyPM["PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE"]
        pmA[Equipment Operating] --> pmB{Scheduled Interval Reached?}
        pmB -- Yes --> pmC[Planned Shutdown]
        pmC --> pmD[Routine Service / Replacement]
        pmD --> pmA
        pmB -- No --> pmA
    end
    subgraph StrategyPdM["PREDICTIVE MAINTENANCE"]
        pdA[Equipment Operating with Sensors] --> pdB{Condition Threshold Exceeded?}
        pdB -- Yes --> pdC[Targeted Maintenance]
        pdC --> pdD[Repair Only the Degrading Component]
        pdD --> pdA
        pdB -- No --> pdA
    end
```

### 4.3 Cost-Time Trade-off Schematic (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Time Horizon] --> Y[Strategy Cost Curve]
    Y --> Z1[Breakdown: Low Initial, High Long-Run]
    Y --> Z2[Preventive: Moderate Initial, Moderate Long-Run]
    Y --> Z3[Predictive: High Initial, Lowest Long-Run]
    Z1 --> W[Crossover Point]
    Z2 --> W
    Z3 --> W
    W --> V[Optimal Strategy for Long-Term Operations]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Breakdown Maintenance appears cheapest at the start because no proactive investment is needed. However, as time progresses, unplanned failures accumulate and total cost grows steeply. Predictive Maintenance, although expensive initially due to sensors and software, achieves the lowest total cost over a long operational horizon.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1. Define Breakdown Maintenance and state any two of its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**
Breakdown Maintenance is a corrective maintenance strategy in which repairs are carried out only after an equipment has failed or its performance has dropped below acceptable limits. It is also known as reactive or run-to-failure maintenance.

Limitations:
- Unplanned downtime disrupts production schedules and reduces overall equipment effectiveness.
- Emergency repairs cost significantly more than planned maintenance and may cause secondary damage to adjacent components.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Preventive Maintenance and Predictive Maintenance.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Preventive Maintenance | Predictive Maintenance |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Trigger | Fixed time or usage interval | Real-time condition data |
| Basis | Calendar schedule | Sensor-based diagnostics |
| Cost | Moderate | Higher initial, lower long-run |
| Intervention | Often replaces parts that still have life | Replaces only parts showing degradation |

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the three types of maintenance strategies with suitable industrial examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three major types of maintenance strategies are:

1. **Breakdown Maintenance (BM):** In this strategy, no maintenance action is taken until the equipment fails. Repairs are performed after the failure occurs. It is suitable for non-critical, low-cost assets where the cost of preventive inspection exceeds the cost of repair. Example: Office air-conditioners, basic hand tools, standby lighting systems.

2. **Preventive Maintenance (PM):** In this strategy, maintenance is carried out at predetermined intervals regardless of the equipment's actual condition. It reduces the probability of unexpected failure by replacing parts before their expected wear-out. Example: Servicing a car every 5,000 km, replacing turbine blades every 10,000 hours.

3. **Predictive Maintenance (PdM):** This is a condition-based strategy that uses continuous monitoring (vibration, temperature, oil analysis) to detect early signs of degradation. Maintenance is performed only when indicators show that failure is imminent. Example: Vibration monitoring of rolling mill bearings, thermographic inspection of switchgear.

**(b)** A hydraulic press operates with an MTBF of 1200 hours and an MTTR of 10 hours. Calculate (i) the steady-state availability, (ii) the reliability for a 200-hour continuous operation, and (iii) the expected annual downtime for 300 working days of 8 hours each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\text{MTBF} = 1200 \text{ hours}$
- $\text{MTTR} = 10 \text{ hours}$
- Total operating hours per year = $300 \times 8 = 2400 \text{ hours}$
- Mission time $t = 200 \text{ hours}$

**(i) Steady-State Availability:**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{1200}{1200 + 10} = \frac{1200}{1210} = 0.9917
$$

$$
A\% \approx 99.17\%
$$

**(ii) Reliability for 200-Hour Mission:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{1200} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}
$$

$$
R(200) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(8.333 \times 10^{-4}) \times 200} = e^{-0.1667}
$$

$$
R(200) = 0.8465 \approx 84.65\%
$$

**(iii) Expected Annual Downtime:**

$$
\text{Failures per Year} = \frac{2400}{1200} = 2 \text{ failures}
$$

$$
\text{Annual Downtime} = 2 \times 10 = 20 \text{ hours/year}
$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of Predictive Maintenance over Preventive Maintenance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages of Predictive Maintenance over Preventive Maintenance:**

1. **Reduced Unnecessary Maintenance:** PdM intervenes only when sensors indicate actual degradation, whereas PM replaces parts on a fixed schedule regardless of their condition. This eliminates wasteful replacement of parts with remaining useful life.

2. **Lower Long-Run Cost:** Although PdM has higher initial investment in sensors and analytics, the total cost of ownership is significantly lower due to fewer interventions and reduced spare parts inventory.

3. **Minimized Unplanned Downtime:** PdM detects incipient failures well in advance, allowing planned corrective action and avoiding catastrophic breakdowns.

4. **Improved Safety:** Continuous monitoring of critical parameters (temperature, vibration, pressure) provides early warning of hazardous conditions, enhancing workplace safety.

5. **Better Spare Parts Management:** Just-in-time procurement is enabled because failures can be forecast accurately, reducing inventory carrying costs.

**Disadvantages of Predictive Maintenance:**

1. **High Initial Capital Investment:** Sensors, data acquisition systems, and analytics software require substantial upfront spending.

2. **Requires Skilled Personnel:** Data interpretation demands trained analysts who can distinguish between normal variation and genuine degradation signals.

3. **Not Universally Applicable:** PdM is ineffective for assets where failure modes cannot be measured through sensors or where degradation is sudden rather than gradual.

**(b)** Compare Breakdown, Preventive, and Predictive Maintenance strategies on the basis of cost, downtime, planning, and applicability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The following comparative table summarizes the three strategies across the requested dimensions:

| Parameter | Breakdown Maintenance | Preventive Maintenance | Predictive Maintenance |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Initial Cost | Very low | Moderate | High |
| Operating Cost | High (long-run) | Moderate | Low (long-run) |
| Downtime | Unplanned, severe | Planned, moderate | Minimal, targeted |
| Planning Level | None | High | Very high |
| Data Requirement | None | Low | Continuous |
| Skill Level Required | Basic repair | Trained technician | Analyst + technician |
| Suitable Assets | Non-critical, low-cost | Moderate-critical | High-critical, expensive |
| Risk of Over-Maintenance | None | High | Very low |
| Risk of Unexpected Failure | Very high | Low | Very low |

**Synthesis:** For long-term industrial operations, organizations typically follow a **tiered approach** — using Breakdown Maintenance for non-critical auxiliary assets, Preventive Maintenance for general machinery, and Predictive Maintenance for high-value critical equipment such as turbines, compressors, and CNC machines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing MTBF with MTTR:** Students frequently swap the two in the availability formula. **Remember:** MTBF is in the numerator (good time), MTTR is in the denominator addition (bad time).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert to percentage:** Writing $A = 0.99$ instead of $A = 99\%$ costs a full mark in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Mixing reliability with availability:** Reliability $R(t)$ is a probability (0 to 1, dimensionless) for a *specific mission time*, while availability is a *long-run fraction* of operational time.
> 4. **Skipping units:** Always mention "hours" for MTBF and MTTR. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** When using $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, explicitly state that the failure rate is assumed constant (exponential distribution).

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Bullets for Last-Minute Revision:**
> - **BM** = Reactive; cheapest initially but disastrous long-term.
> - **PM** = Time-based; replaces parts on schedule regardless of condition.
> - **PdM** = Condition-based; replaces parts only when sensors indicate degradation.
> - **Availability Formula:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$
> - **Reliability Formula:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}}$
> - **Failure Rate:** $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$
> - **PdM Techniques:** Vibration, Thermography, Oil Analysis, Ultrasonic, Acoustic Emission.
> - **Best Long-Run Strategy:** Tiered approach — BM for non-critical, PM for general, PdM for critical assets.
> - **Critical Distinction:** Availability is a *time-average*, while Reliability is a *mission-specific probability*.
> - **Examiner's Rule:** Always state units, assumptions, and formula source for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lean Manufacturing (LM): Basic Elements - Tools - Concept of Wastes - Stages of 5S and Waste Elimination - Need for LM - Toyota Production System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT614 - PRINCIPLES OF INDUSTRIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lean Manufacturing (LM): Basic Elements - Tools - Concept of Wastes - Stages of 5S and Waste Elimination - Need for LM - Toyota Production System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lean Manufacturing (LM)** is a systematic, philosophy-driven production methodology that aims to minimize waste (*Muda*), inconsistency (*Mura*), and overburden (*Muri*) in manufacturing processes, while maximizing value creation for the end customer. Originally derived from the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**, it is formally defined by the KTU 2024 curriculum as an integrated socio-technical system whose objective is to deliver the right product, in the right quantity, with the right quality, at the right time, at the lowest possible cost, by continuously eliminating non-value-adding activities across the entire value stream.

The term *Lean* was coined by **John Krafcik** in his 1988 MIT thesis, later popularized by **James P. Womack and Daniel T. Jones** in the seminal 1990 book *"The Machine That Changed the World"*. The philosophy is built on the foundational belief that any activity that consumes resources but does not create value for the customer is a candidate for elimination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Terminology (Must Memorize):**
> - **Value (Gemba Value):** Anything the customer is willing to pay for.
> - **Value Stream:** The complete set of activities required to take a product from raw material to the customer's hands.
> - **Flow:** The smooth, uninterrupted movement of products/services through the value stream.
> - **Pull:** Producing only what the customer demands, when they demand it.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Lean Kitchen**
> Imagine a home kitchen where you are cooking dinner for guests. A **Lean Kitchen** works like this: you pre-chop only the vegetables you need (avoiding **overproduction**), the ingredients move from the fridge to the cutting board to the pan without sitting in piles (avoiding **waiting/transportation**), the recipe is simple and clear (avoiding **over-processing**), and the cook has exactly the right tools ready (organized workspace — the **5S principle**). A **Traditional Kitchen** is cluttered with unused gadgets, half-open spice jars, leftover ingredients, and a chef running back and forth. Lean Manufacturing is simply the disciplined engineering of the *Lean Kitchen* for the entire factory floor.

The intuitive takeaway: **Lean is not about making workers faster; it is about removing the obstacles that prevent them from being fast.** It is a *systemic* and *cultural* transformation, not merely a toolkit.

### 1.3 Why Lean Manufacturing Matters (KTU Context)

| Dimension | Traditional Manufacturing | Lean Manufacturing |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost Mindset** | Cost-plus (push) | Target costing (pull) |
| **Quality** | Inspect-in quality | Build-in quality (*Jidoka*) |
| **Inventory** | Buffer safety stock | Just-in-Time (JIT) flow |
| **Improvement** | Periodic, top-down | Continuous (*Kaizen*), bottom-up |
| **Focus** | Departmental silos | End-to-end value stream |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Basic Elements (Foundational Pillars) of Lean Manufacturing

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly highlights **five core elements** that constitute the philosophical foundation of Lean:

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5 Basic Elements of Lean:**
> 1. **Value (Value Identification)** — Defining what the customer actually values from their perspective.
> 2. **Value Stream (Value Stream Mapping - VSM)** — Identifying every action (value-adding vs. non-value-adding) required to deliver the product.
> 3. **Flow** — Ensuring the remaining value-adding steps occur in a continuous, uninterrupted sequence without stoppages, queues, or backflows.
> 4. **Pull** — Letting the customer *pull* value from the producer; the upstream process produces only what the downstream process signals.
> 5. **Perfection (Continuous Improvement)** — Relentlessly pursuing perfection by continuously removing layers of waste as they are uncovered.

These five elements form a strict sequential dependency. Skipping any one breaks the system: *Value* must be defined before the *Value Stream* can be mapped; *Value Stream* must be mapped before *Flow* can be designed; *Flow* must exist before *Pull* can function; and *Pull* generates the visibility needed to pursue *Perfection*.

### 2.2 Lean Tools (KTU High-Yield List)

Lean Manufacturing is operationalized through a portfolio of standardized tools. The KTU board examiner frequently tests the following:

| # | Tool | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **5S** | Workplace organization & standardization |
| 2 | **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** | Visualize material & information flow |
| 3 | **Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)** | Small, incremental, daily improvements |
| 4 | **Kanban** | Visual pull-based signaling system |
| 5 | **Poka-Yoke** | Mistake-proofing devices |
| 6 | **Jidoka (Autonomation)** | Automation with human touch — stop on defect |
| 7 | **Heijunka** | Level scheduling / production leveling |
| 8 | **SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die)** | Reduce changeover/setup time below 10 minutes |
| 9 | **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** | Zero breakdowns, zero defects |
| 10 | **Andon** | Visual signal for abnormality at the workplace |
| 11 | **Gemba Walks** | Going to the actual place where work happens |
| 12 | **A3 Thinking** | Structured problem-solving report |

### 2.3 The Concept of Wastes (Muda) — The Heart of Lean

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WASTE = Any activity that consumes resources but creates NO value for the customer.**

The original Toyota framework identified **7 Wastes (TIMWOODS)**. Later, the **8th Waste** (*Unused Human Talent / Creativity of Employees*) was added by Womack & Jones. KTU examiners expect students to know all eight and be able to give **manufacturing examples** for each.

#### The 8 Wastes Framework

| # | Waste (English) | Japanese Term | Meaning | Real Manufacturing Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transportation** | *Kotsu* | Unnecessary movement of materials/products | Moving parts between distant warehouses |
| 2 | **Inventory** | *Zaiko* | Excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods | Piles of unsold stock blocking aisles |
| 3 | **Motion** | *Undo* | Unnecessary movement of *people* | Worker bending repeatedly to pick bolts |
| 4 | **Waiting** | *Teido* | Idle time of people, machines, or materials | Machine down, operator standing idle |
| 5 | **Overproduction** | *Murier* | Producing more/earlier than customer needs | Batch production beyond orders |
| 6 | **Over-processing** | *Furyou* | Doing more work than the customer requires | Painting an invisible part of a product |
| 7 | **Defects** | *Hifuu* | Production of faulty goods requiring rework | Scrapped castings requiring remelting |
| 8 | **Unused Talent (Human Potential)** | *Chihou* | Wasting skills, ideas, & creativity of workers | Supervisor ignores operator improvement idea |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Valuation):** Confusing *Transportation* (movement of *materials*) with *Motion* (movement of *people*). These are **two different wastes**. Examiners deduct marks for this confusion.

### 2.4 The 5S Methodology — Five Stages of Workplace Discipline

> [!NOTE]
> **5S** is a Lean tool for workplace organization originating in post-WWII Japan. It is a prerequisite for all higher-level Lean tools. Each *S* is a Japanese word beginning with *S*, paired with an English equivalent.

The five stages, in **strict sequential order**, are:

| Stage | Japanese | English | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ***Seiri*** | **Sort** | Separate needed vs. unneeded items; discard the latter | Only essentials remain |
| 2 | ***Seiton*** | **Set in Order** | Arrange items for *easy* access; "a place for everything, and everything in its place" | Visual workplace |
| 3 | ***Seiso*** | **Shine** | Clean the workspace; inspect while cleaning | Equipment & floor are spotless |
| 4 | ***Seiketsu*** | **Standardize** | Establish standards & procedures to sustain the first three *S* | Repeatable system |
| 5 | ***Shitsuke*** | **Sustain** | Self-discipline to follow the established 5S habits continuously | 5S becomes a culture |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **5S as a Foundation:** The 5S framework is the *gateway* Lean tool — without an organized workplace, no higher Lean tool (VSM, Kanban, SMED) can function effectively. KTU examiners love to ask: *"Why is 5S considered the foundation of Lean?"*

### 2.5 The Toyota Production System (TPS) — The Mother of Lean

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Toyota Production System (TPS)** is the original production system developed by Toyota Motor Corporation (Japan) between 1948 and 1975, primarily engineered by **Taiichi Ohno** (the father of TPS) and refined with input from **Sakichi Toyoda** (founder of Toyota Industries). TPS is the *cultural and engineering origin* of Lean Manufacturing.

#### TPS House — The "House of Toyota"

The TPS is best understood as a **house**:
- **Roof:** The goal — *Best Quality, Lowest Cost, Shortest Lead Time, High Morale, High Safety*.
- **Two Pillars:**
  1. ***Jidoka*** (Autonomation) — Automation with a human touch. Stop the line when a defect is detected.
  2. ***Just-in-Time (JIT)*** — Produce only what is needed, only when it is needed, only in the amount needed.
- **Foundation:** ***Heijunka*** (Production Leveling) + *Standardized Work* + *5S*.
- **Heart of the House:** ***Kaizen*** (Continuous Improvement) + *Respect for People*.

> [!NOTE]
> **The TPS Equation:**
>
> $$\text{TPS} = \text{JIT} \times \text{Jidoka} \times \text{Kaizen} \times \text{Respect for People}$$
>
> Removing any one of the four factors collapses the house. This is a **favorite KTU question** — "Explain the TPS House with a neat diagram."

### 2.6 Need for Lean Manufacturing (KTU Frequent Question)

> [!TIP]
> **Why does industry need Lean? (Examiner's model 4-point answer):**
> 1. **Global Competition** — Survival requires lower cost, higher quality, faster delivery.
> 2. **Customer Demand for Variety** — Customers want customized, high-variety, low-volume products.
> 3. **Resource Scarcity** — Wasted materials, energy, and labor directly erode profitability and sustainability.
> 4. **Elimination of Non-Value-Added Activities** — In a typical factory, **only 5% of total activities add value**; the rest (95%) is waste. Lean targets this 95%.

### 2.7 KTU Formula / Metrics Sheet (Lean Indicators)

| Metric | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Lean Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lead Time (LT)** | $LT = PT + WIP\,Queue$ | $PT$ = Processing Time, $WIP\,Queue$ = Wait time | Minimize LT for fixed PT |
| **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** | $VAR = \dfrac{VA\,Time}{Total\,Lead\,Time} \times 100\%$ | $VA$ = Value-Added | $\geq 25\%$ is excellent |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{Good\,Units\,at\,First\,Attempt}{Total\,Units\,Started} \times 100\%$ | — | $\geq 99\%$ |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality | $\geq 85\%$ (World Class) |
| **Takt Time** | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{Available\,Production\,Time}{Customer\,Demand}$ | — | Matches customer demand rate |
| **Inventory Turns (IT)** | $IT = \dfrac{COGS}{Average\,Inventory}$ | $COGS$ = Cost of Goods Sold | Maximize turns |
| **Setup Time Reduction** | $\%\,Reduction = \dfrac{T_{before} - T_{after}}{T_{before}} \times 100\%$ | — | $\geq 50\%$ typical SMED target |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute-value and division expressions use `\vert` / `\mid` / `\dfrac` to ensure clean KTU-format printing. Never use `|x|` or `|` inside markdown table cells.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Calculating the Value-Added Ratio (VAR)

The **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** is the *single most important Lean metric* tested in KTU numerical problems. Below is a full step-by-step evaluation.

#### Problem Setup
A manufacturing cell has the following activities for one unit of product:
- Processing Time at Machine A: **5 min**
- Wait before Machine B: **12 min**
- Processing Time at Machine B: **8 min**
- Inspection Time: **3 min**
- Wait before Machine C: **10 min**
- Processing Time at Machine C: **4 min**
- Rework due to defect: **6 min**

#### Step 1: Identify Value-Added (VA) Activities

A *Value-Added* activity must satisfy **all three** criteria:
1. The customer **wants** the transformation.
2. The transformation is **done right the first time**.
3. The activity is **physically and materially necessary**.

| Activity | Time (min) | VA? | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Machine A processing | 5 | **Yes** | Transforms raw → semi-finished (required) |
| Wait before B | 12 | **No** | Pure queue waste (*Waiting*) |
| Machine B processing | 8 | **Yes** | Required transformation |
| Inspection | 3 | **No** | *Over-processing* — checking what should not have been produced wrong |
| Wait before C | 10 | **No** | Pure queue waste (*Waiting*) |
| Machine C processing | 4 | **Yes** | Required transformation |
| Rework | 6 | **No** | *Defect* waste; the original was non-conforming |

#### Step 2: Compute Total Lead Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
LT &= 5 + 12 + 8 + 3 + 10 + 4 + 6 \\
LT &= 48 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Compute Total Value-Added Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
VA_{time} &= 5 + 8 + 4 \\
VA_{time} &= 17 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Compute the Value-Added Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAR &= \frac{VA_{time}}{LT} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{17}{48} \times 100\% \\
&= 35.42\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** A VAR of **35.42%** means **64.58%** of the lead time is *waste*. Lean target in a world-class facility: **VAR $\geq$ 25%** (still excellent because 75% of the time was non-VA — highlighting the magnitude of waste in industry).

#### Step 5: Categorize the Wastes (Cross-Reference to 8 Wastes)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{Waiting} &= 12 + 10 = 22 \text{ min} \\
W_{OverProcessing} &= 3 \text{ min} \\
W_{Defects} &= 6 \text{ min} \\
\text{Total Waste} &= 22 + 3 + 6 = 31 \text{ min} \\
\text{Verification: } LT - VA &= 48 - 17 = 31 \text{ min} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: The 5S Scoring Rubric (for KTU Audit-type questions)

| Stage | Question Audit Checkpoint | Score 0 | Score 1 | Score 2 | Score 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ***Seiri* (Sort)** | Are only necessary items at the workplace? | Many unwanted items | Some | Few | None |
| ***Seiton* (Set in Order)** | Can anyone find a tool in $\leq 30$ seconds? | Cannot find | $\leq 5$ min | $\leq 1$ min | $\leq 30$ sec |
| ***Seiso* (Shine)** | Is the floor & machine clean? | Dirty | Partly clean | Mostly clean | Spotless |
| ***Seiketsu* (Standardize)** | Are visual standards & SOPs posted? | None | A few | Most | All |
| ***Shitsuke* (Sustain)** | Is 5S followed without supervision? | No | Sometimes | Often | Always |

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Maximum Score = 15.** A score of $\geq 12$ indicates a *Lean-ready* workplace; $< 8$ indicates the cell is **not yet ready** for higher Lean tools.

### 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix: TPS vs. Mass Production

| Parameter | Mass Production (Ford) | Toyota Production System (TPS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Philosophy** | Produce as much as cheaply as possible | Produce only what the customer needs |
| **Volume** | High volume, low variety | High variety, low-to-mid volume |
| **Lead Time** | Long (buffer stock) | Short (flow + pull) |
| **Inventory** | High safety stock | Minimal (JIT) |
| **Quality Control** | End-of-line inspection | Build-in quality (*Jidoka*) |
| **Improvement** | Periodic | Continuous (*Kaizen*) |
| **Human Role** | Do as told | Suggest improvements (Respect for People) |
| **Defect Handling** | Rework & ship | Stop the line (*Andon*) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The TPS House (Toyota Production System)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    roof["ROOF: Best Quality, Lowest Cost, Shortest Lead Time, Safety, Morale"]
    
    jidoka["PILLAR 1: JIDOKA (Autonomation - Stop on Defect)"]
    jit["PILLAR 2: JUST-IN-TIME (Right Item, Right Time, Right Quantity)"]
    
    heijunka["FOUNDATION BLOCK: Heijunka - Production Leveling"]
    stdwork["FOUNDATION BLOCK: Standardized Work"]
    fiveS["FOUNDATION BLOCK: 5S Workplace Discipline"]
    qc["FOUNDATION BLOCK: Quality Circles"]
    
    kaizen["HEART: KAIZEN - Continuous Improvement"]
    respect["HEART: RESPECT FOR PEOPLE"]
    
    roof --> jidoka
    roof --> jit
    jidoka --- heijunka
    jidoka --- stdwork
    jidoka --- fiveS
    jidoka --- qc
    jit --- heijunka
    jit --- stdwork
    jit --- fiveS
    jit --- qc
    kaizen --- jidoka
    kaizen --- jit
    respect --- jidoka
    respect --- jit
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the House:** The *roof* (goal) rests on the *two pillars* (Jidoka + JIT), which rest on the *foundation* (Heijunka, Std Work, 5S, Quality Circles). The *heart* of the house is the *engine* — Kaizen + Respect for People — that drives everything.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The 5S Sequential Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["STAGE 1: SEIRI (SORT) - Remove unnecessary items"]
    s2["STAGE 2: SEITON (SET IN ORDER) - Arrange essentials"]
    s3["STAGE 3: SEISO (SHINE) - Clean the workplace"]
    s4["STAGE 4: SEIKETSU (STANDARDIZE) - Document standards"]
    s5["STAGE 5: SHITSUKE (SUSTAIN) - Build discipline"]
    
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 -.Continuous Audits & Training.-> s1
    
    wasteA["WASTE REDUCTION"] 
    s1 -. Eliminates .-> wasteA
    s2 -. Reduces .-> wasteA
    s3 -. Detects .-> wasteA
    s4 -. Prevents .-> wasteA
    s5 -. Sustains .-> wasteA
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: The 8 Wastes (DOWNTIME Framework)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    central["THE 8 WASTES (Muda) - Customer-Perspective Non-Value-Added"]
    
    D["D - DEFECTS: Rework, scrap, warranty"]
    O["O - OVERPRODUCTION: Producing more than needed"]
    W1["W - WAITING: Idle time of people/machines"]
    N["N - NON-UTILIZED TALENT: Wasting human potential"]
    T["T - TRANSPORTATION: Moving materials unnecessarily"]
    I["I - INVENTORY: Excess stock, WIP buildup"]
    M["M - MOTION: Unnecessary human movement"]
    E["E - EXTRA-PROCESSING: Doing more than customer wants"]
    
    central --- D
    central --- O
    central --- W1
    central --- N
    central --- T
    central --- I
    central --- M
    central --- E
    
    counter["LEUNTERVENTION: 5S, Kaizen, Kanban, VSM, Poka-Yoke, Jidoka, TPM"]
    D --> counter
    O --> counter
    W1 --> counter
    N --> counter
    T --> counter
    I --> counter
    M --> counter
    E --> counter
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Lean Implementation Roadmap (for KTU 14-mark question)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA["STEP 1: Identify Value from Customer's Voice (VOC)"]
    stepB["STEP 2: Map the Current State Value Stream (VSM)"]
    stepC["STEP 3: Design the Future State VSM"]
    stepD["STEP 4: Implement 5S as Foundation"]
    stepE["STEP 5: Establish Flow using Heijunka and SMED"]
    stepF["STEP 6: Introduce Pull via Kanban"]
    stepG["STEP 7: Install Jidoka and Poka-Yoke"]
    stepH["STEP 8: Sustain via Kaizen and TPM"]
    
    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH -.feedback loop.-> stepA
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to draw this in the exam:** Use a simple *block-flow* diagram with arrows. Label each block clearly. Mention the *feedback loop* from Step 8 → Step 1 to indicate continuous improvement. This fetches **full marks** in the KTU valuation key.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define Lean Manufacturing. List any four basic elements of Lean.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** Lean Manufacturing is a systematic production methodology that aims to eliminate waste (*Muda*), inconsistency (*Mura*), and overburden (*Muri*) in all business processes, delivering maximum value to the customer with minimum resources.
**Four Basic Elements (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Value** — Specify value from the customer's perspective.
2. **Value Stream** — Map the entire end-to-end value-creating process.
3. **Flow** — Make value-creating steps flow without interruption.
4. **Pull** — Let the customer pull value; produce only on demand.
*(5th element — Perfection — may be added for half a mark if expanded.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Briefly explain the 5S methodology and state its importance in Lean Manufacturing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Meaning (1 mark):** 5S is a workplace organization methodology with five Japanese steps — *Seiri* (Sort), *Seiton* (Set in Order), *Seiso* (Shine), *Seiketsu* (Standardize), and *Shitsuke* (Sustain).
**Importance (2 marks — any two):**
1. Creates a clean, organized, and safe workplace.
2. Reduces time wasted searching for tools and materials.
3. Acts as the **foundation** upon which all higher Lean tools (VSM, Kanban, SMED) are built.
4. Helps detect abnormalities (e.g., oil leaks) early, supporting *Jidoka* and *Kaizen*.

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Module 2, ESE Question**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

> **(a)** Explain the **8 Wastes (TIMWOODS + Unused Talent)** of Lean Manufacturing with one manufacturing example for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** with a neat diagram of the TPS House. Explain the role of *Jidoka* and *Just-in-Time* as the two pillars. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

| Waste | Example (1 mark each — 0.5 for definition + 0.5 for example) |
|---|---|
| **1. Transportation** — Unnecessary movement of materials/products | Moving semi-finished parts from one building to another inside a plant |
| **2. Inventory** — Excess raw material, WIP, finished goods | Stocking a 6-month inventory of bolts that get used in 2 weeks |
| **3. Motion** — Unnecessary movement of *people* | Operator walking 50 m to fetch a frequently used tool |
| **4. Waiting** — Idle time of people/machines/materials | A CNC machine sits idle for 20 min waiting for the operator to set it up |
| **5. Overproduction** — Producing more/earlier than needed | Producing 10,000 units when only 2,000 are ordered this month |
| **6. Over-processing** — Doing more than the customer requires | Polishing a hidden internal surface of a gearbox that the customer never sees |
| **7. Defects** — Producing faulty products requiring rework | 5% of castings are rejected → requires remelting → wasted energy & labor |
| **8. Unused Talent** — Wasting skills/ideas of employees | Supervisor ignores a worker suggestion to mount a tray on the machine → output ↑ but suggestion is never implemented |

*(0.75 mark per waste; total 6 marks. 1 mark reserved for a clear introductory sentence + a conclusion such as "Eliminating these 8 wastes is the central objective of Lean Manufacturing.")*

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**TPS Definition (1 mark):** The Toyota Production System is the original *Lean* production system developed by Toyota Motor Corporation (Japan) under the leadership of **Taiichi Ohno** between 1948 and 1975. It is designed to deliver the highest quality at the lowest cost and shortest lead time.

**TPS House Diagram (3 marks — must include):**
- **Roof:** Best Quality, Lowest Cost, Shortest Lead Time, Safety, Morale
- **Two Pillars:** *Jidoka* and *Just-in-Time*
- **Foundation:** *Heijunka*, *Standardized Work*, *5S*
- **Heart/Engine:** *Kaizen* + *Respect for People*

**Role of Pillars (3 marks — 1.5 each):**

**1. Jidoka (Autonomation):** Means *automation with a human touch*. The machine *automatically detects* a defect (or the worker pulls the *Andon* cord) and **stops the line immediately** so that the defect cannot pass downstream. This prevents mass production of defective goods, supports build-in quality, and exposes the root cause of problems.

**2. Just-in-Time (JIT):** Produces *only what is needed, only when it is needed, and only in the amount needed*. JIT minimizes inventory, eliminates overproduction, and exposes waste. It works through the *Kanban* pull system, where every upstream process produces only the quantity withdrawn by the downstream customer-process.

**Conclusion (0 marks extra but recommended for full marks):** Jidoka prevents bad units from being made, while JIT prevents too many units from being made. Together, they form the two pillars of the TPS House.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

> **(a)** Describe the **5S methodology** in detail. Explain how each *S* contributes to waste elimination in a manufacturing plant. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A CNC cell takes **120 minutes** to manufacture a single component. The activity breakdown is: VA Processing = 35 min, Inspection = 8 min, Waiting = 45 min, Internal Transport = 12 min, Rework = 14 min, and Other = 6 min. **(i)** Calculate the **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** and **(ii)** Identify the **8 wastes** that apply, with the time lost to each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

| Stage | Japanese | Meaning | Contribution to Waste Elimination (1.5 marks for all five) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ***Seiri*** (Sort) | Separate needed vs. unneeded items; discard the rest | Eliminates **Inventory** waste, **Motion** waste, and **Transportation** waste by removing clutter |
| 2 | ***Seiton*** (Set in Order) | "A place for everything, and everything in its place" | Reduces **Motion** and **Waiting** waste — workers find tools in seconds |
| 3 | ***Seiso*** (Shine) | Clean the workplace & equipment; inspect while cleaning | Detects **Defects** early (oil leaks, loose bolts) — supports *Jidoka* |
| 4 | ***Seiketsu*** (Standardize) | Create standards, SOPs, and visual signals for the first 3S | Prevents backsliding to old habits; sustains the improvements |
| 5 | ***Shitsuke*** (Sustain) | Self-discipline to follow 5S as a daily habit | Makes 5S a *culture*; enables higher Lean tools (Kanban, SMED) to function |

*(1.4 marks for the table; 0.6 mark for a concluding sentence — "5S is the foundation of all Lean tools.")*

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Compute Total Lead Time (0.5 mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
LT &= 35 + 8 + 45 + 12 + 14 + 6 \\
LT &= 120 \text{ minutes (as given)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Value-Added Time (0.5 mark)**
Only the *Processing* time is truly value-added from the customer's perspective.
$$
VA_{time} = 35 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3: Compute VAR (1 mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
VAR &= \frac{VA_{time}}{LT} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{35}{120} \times 100\% \\
&= 29.17\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Classify the Wastes (4 marks — 0.5 per waste, 8 wastes × 0.5 = 4 marks; only those present count)**

| Activity | Time (min) | Waste Category (8-Waste framework) |
|---|---|---|
| Inspection (8 min) | 8 | **Over-processing** (checking what should be right by design) |
| Waiting (45 min) | 45 | **Waiting** |
| Internal Transport (12 min) | 12 | **Transportation** |
| Rework (14 min) | 14 | **Defects** |
| Other (6 min) | 6 | Could include **Motion** (operator movement) or **Over-processing** |
| **Total Non-Value-Added** | **85 min** | (70.83% of total time is waste) |

**Step 5: Conclusion (1 mark)**
With a VAR of only **29.17%**, the cell is performing reasonably by industry standards but still has **85 minutes of waste per 120-minute cycle**. Lean tools such as SMED (to reduce waiting/setup), Kanban (to reduce inventory), and Poka-Yoke (to reduce defects) should be deployed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Misclassifying Inspection as "Value-Added"** — Inspection is *Over-processing* in the 8-Waste framework. Marks are deducted if students mark it as VA.
> 2. **Conflating Transportation & Motion** — Transportation = material movement; Motion = people movement. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the 8th Waste (Unused Talent)** — KTU frequently tests this; missing it loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the TPS House diagram** — In any question worth $\geq 10$ marks, the diagram is **mandatory**. Without it, examiner deducts 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Giving 5S in wrong order** — *Seiri → Seiton → Seiso → Seiketsu → Shitsuke*. The sequence is non-negotiable. Reversing or skipping loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Numerical: Forgetting to multiply by 100% in VAR** — Many students write `35/120 = 0.29` and stop. Always write as a **percentage** unless the question specifies a fraction.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 2):**

- [x] **Lean Manufacturing** = systematic waste elimination + value maximization (Womack & Jones / Taiichi Ohno).
- [x] **5 Basic Elements** (in order): **Value → Value Stream → Flow → Pull → Perfection.**
- [x] **5S** (in order): **Seiri → Seiton → Seiso → Seiketsu → Shitsuke.** *Sustain is the hardest stage.*
- [x] **8 Wastes (TIMWOODS + Talent):** **T**ransportation, **I**nventory, **M**otion, **W**aiting, **O**verproduction, **O**ver-processing, **D**efects, **U**nused Talent. (Mnemonic: **DOWNTIME** — Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilized talent, Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Extra-processing.)
- [x] **Toyota Production System** — Taiichi Ohno (1948–1975). Two pillars = **Jidoka + JIT**. Foundation = Heijunka + Std Work + 5S. Engine = Kaizen + Respect for People.
- [x] **Jidoka** = automation with a human touch; **stop the line on defect.**
- [x] **JIT (Just-in-Time)** = right item, right time, right quantity; pull system via **Kanban.**
- [x] **5S is the foundation** of all other Lean tools — without 5S, no Kaizen, Kanban, or VSM will work.
- [x] **Key Metrics:** VAR = $VA_{time} / LT \times 100\%$; Takt Time = Available Time / Customer Demand; OEE = $A \times P \times Q$.
- [x] **Why Lean is needed:** Global competition, customer variety demand, resource scarcity, and the fact that **95% of activities in a typical factory are non-value-added**.
- [x] **Lean is a culture, not a tool kit** — it requires *Respect for People* and *continuous improvement (Kaizen)* as the engine.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
